speech: Hi, yeah, thanks for having me. So longest substring without repeating characters. I've seen this one before, kind of the classic sliding window thing, I think.
Let me restate it. We need the max length of a subsequence—sorry, substring—where all characters are unique. Not subsequence, substring, contiguous.

My first instinct is nested loops: for every start index, expand until we see a repeat and track the max. That's probably like n squared, which might be fine for interviews sometimes, but I'll optimize after.
Inner loop: if character already in seen, we should... actually I'll add it first then check—wait, I'll check before adding. If seen has s of j, break. Otherwise add and update max.
Hmm. Actually is j minus i right? When j equals i that's zero but we have one character. So maybe it should be j minus i plus one. I'll change that.
Time complexity: outer n inner n so n squared. Space, the set could be up to alphabet size, so constant if we assume ASCII, roughly.
Can we do better? Sliding window with two pointers. Left and right. We move right, if duplicate we move left until it's valid again. I'll use a map from char to index.
If last of s at right exists and is greater than or equal to left, set left to last plus one. Then store last of s at right equals right. Max length is max of maxLen and right minus left.
Wait, I need to handle when last of c is undefined. In JavaScript undefined compared to number might be weird. I'll use if last c is not undefined and last c greater or equal left.
Actually for the length, when right and left are both zero we have one character so it should be right minus left plus one. Let me fix that.
Complexity now is O of n log n) because the map operations—actually hash map is O(1) average so linear time, O of n. Space is O of min n, alphabet).
Let me trace p w w k e w mentally. When second w, we jump left... should give three for w k e. I think that's right.
Interviewer: What about empty string? Me: Oh, the loops just don't run, maxLen stays zero, return zero. Good.
Could also use a Set and while loop to shrink from the left—same idea. I like the last index version because it's one pass.
If the string had only spaces or unicode, the map still works, keys are strings. Space complexity is O of n) in worst case all unique—wait that's still bounded by length of s.
Testing: b b b b b should return 1. My code... yeah maxLen updates each step. For a b c a b c, longest is a b c length three.
One more edge: single character string. Should be 1. Right zero, left zero, plus one gives one. Cool.
I'm trying to think if there's a bug with zero-based index when we jump left. If duplicate at same index as left, we set left to last c plus one, window should exclude the old char. Sounds right.
Alternative: for each character we could binary search—no that doesn't apply here. So linear scan is the right idea.
That's my final answer unless you want it in another language. Thanks.
